procedures, abides by the rules of evidence, and follows a
simplified method of procedure modeled after the rules of civil
procedure. Furthermore, li.tigants in APA hearings are entitled
to the minimum standards of notice and a fair hearing before
being deprived of liberty or property interests. California
Administrative Hearinq Practice S 1.3, CEB (1984).

adjudicatory APA hearing which is not covered by the Act. In

applications are clearly adjudicatory by statutory
piescription. Ins. Code S 1851.08. See CaIFarm, 48 Cal.3d at
824.
California cases and statutes create an important
procedural distinction bettreen quasi-judicial and
quasi-legi.slative proceedings. In adjudicatory (or.
guasi-judicial) proceedings like the san Bruno hearings, parties
are afforded certain rights typically attendant to a court
proceeding, such as the opPortunity to . cross-examine witnesses.
these rigtrts are not granted in a quasi-legj.s1ative proceeding.
Moreover, the scope of judicial review of a quasi-judicial
administrative proceeding is different from the scope of
judicial review-for a rulemaking proceedi:ry 7/. Judicial review
ior adjudicatory actions is governed by administrative mandamus,
under a "substantial evidence" standard of review, while
judicial review of quasi-legislative actions is governed by
ordinary mandamus, to determine whether the agency acted
arbitraiily or capriciously, with no evidentiary support, or did
not comply with piescribed procedures. See.Cal' Ci-v. Proc. Code
Section l-094.5(b) (standard for administrative mandamus); cal.

Iilargaret W. E11ison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Cornmission
Uarch 29, 1990
cov't. Code S 11350 (standard for ordinary mandamus); Ri.vera v.
Division of rndustria! We1fare, 255 CaI. App. 2d 585 (1958)
(judic'ia1 review of regulat ions )
To surffnarize, the legislative history and subseguent
application by the FPPC of the term "rate-making proceedinqs"
make clear that the term was meant to apply to a
guasi-legislative form of proceedinqf, and probably only to
"rate-making proceedings" before the PUC. The term was not
meant to apply to hearings like the San Bruno proceedings.
Gener a I 1v
Company-spec i f i c hearings, vrhich will fo]1o!, the
pending Consolidated Hearing, are clearly adjudicatory hearings,
and therefore are not "rate-making proceedings" under the Act.
As di.scussed above, if a rate application is "deemed approved."
there q,iI1 be no heari.ng on that application. Proposition 103
contemplates that a heari.ng will only take place i.f a consumer
or the Commissioner requests a hearing on the rate application.
Such a hearing will be governed by the portion of the APA
dealing 'rrith adjudicatory hearings, and the commissioner must
appoini an ALJ to conduct the hearing' In addi.tion, these
heirings contemplate cross-examinat ion of witnesses and limited
discovery. See Ins. Code S 185].08; Gov't. Code S 11507.5' The
issue in such a hearing would be vrhether the rate proPosed in
the rate application meets the " exces s ivel inadequate " standard
of Proposition 103 and the "fair and reasonable" standard
established in CalFarm.
Thus, this is clearly "quasi-judj-cia1" action, as
defined by the FPPC (see 2 c.C.R. SS 18202(a)(1) ("a proceeding
to determlne the rights or duties of a person under existing
Iaws, regulations or po1lcies") and r8202(a)(z) ("a proceeding
involving the issuance of a lega1 opinion" ) ) . and is not the
t)4le of tituation "where disclosure is intended or justified. "
In re Evans, 4 FPPC Ops. at 92.
5. 
Future Rule and Requlation Makinq
Hear inqs
The Commissioner has suggested that following the
Consolidated Hearing, the Commissioner may hold hearings to
establish rules and regulations for rate rollbacks. Such
hearings will also determine appropriate ranges for general


PETTIT & MARTIN
Margaret W. E1lison, Esq.
Fai, r Political Practices Commission
March 29, I99 0
rates of return. The Commissioner will conduct such hearings i.n
accordance wi.th California Government Code Sections 11340-11446,
which provide the Office of Administrative Law ("OAL") with
jurisdiction over any exercise of quasi-1egis1ati,ve power, with
certain exceptions. 8/
Every state agency which adopts rules and regulations
must submit its newly adopted regulations to the OAL for its
approval. The office must either approve or disapprove a
regulation wi.thin 30 days of its delivery to the OAL, and
fai.lure to act by the office constitutes automatic approval.
Ca1. Gov't Code S r1349.3. In addition, Government Code
Sectj.on 11345.4 reguires a state agency to give the OAL 45 days
notice pri.or to the hearing on adoption of a new rule or
regulation. Thus, no rules or regulations may be issued as a
result of the current Proposition 103 hearings. The
promulgation of any new rules or regulations which may be j.ssued
by the Commissi.oner in the future as a result of Proposition 103
must comply wi.th the OAL's rule and regulation making procedures
This rule making activity is not the same as the
current Consolidated Hearing or any future individual
Application Hearings. As already explained, the Consolidated
Hearing is an administrative hearing before an ALJ who is a
retired, experienced superior court judge. which is akin to a
nonjury trial in a civil court and is governed by the APA,
Government Code Sections 11340-1I528. The APA hearing i-s an
adjudicatory hearing which provides for formal discovery
procedures, abides by the rules of evidence, and follows a
simplified method of procedure modeled after the rules of civil
procedure. Furthermore, li.tigants in APA hearings are entitled
to the minimum standards of notice and a fair hearing before
being deprived of liberty or property interests. California
Administrative Hearinq Practice S 1.3, CEB (1984).
Thus, it is clear that the Consolidated Hearing i.s an
adjudicatory APA hearing which is not covered by the Act. In
contrast. any rule and regulation making hearings which may be
conducted in the future vrith regard to Proposition 103 will be
governed by the OAL, and may potentially trigger filing
requirements under the Act.
B. 
Law Firms and Their Clients Should Not Be
PloYer s
Because the Consolidated Hearing, and hearings on
applications for preliminary approval are not "rate-making

proceedings, " 1aw firms and their clients' activities under
Proposi,tion 103 do not trigger reporting reguirements for
" lobbyi.ng firms" or "lobbyist employers" under the Act.
However, the Corffnittee lvouId like to respond to your question
why 1aw firms and their clients should not be considered
"lobbying firms" or 'lobbyist employers. "
First, lawyers representing their clients in
Proposition 103 hearings should not be considered lobbyists
beciuse of the def ini.tion of "direct communication" in the
California Administrative Code .
A "lobbyist" is defined in Government Code
Section 82039 ( a) as follows:
[A]ny individual who is employed or
contracts for economic consi.deration, other than
reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, to
communicate directly or through his or her
agents with any elective state official . agency
official or legislative official for the purpose
of influencing legislative or admi.nistrative
action, if a substantial or regular portion of
the activi.ties for which he or she receives
consideration is for the purpose of influencing
1egi.s lat ive or administrative action'
This definition contains several key elements. First,
direct communication is defined in the regulations to include
appearing as a witness before, talking to (either by_telephone
oi- in peison), corresponding wi-th or answering questions or
inquiries from any gualifying. official, either personally or
thfough an agent.- Ctris definition does not include-provision of
purelt technicat data or analysis to an. administrati.ve agency by
i perion vrho does not otherwise engage in direct communj"cation
foi ttre purpose of influencing legislatj've or administrative
action.
Further, Section 18239(a) of the regulations expands
upon the issue of direct communication by. specifically excluding
"idministrative testimony" from the description of a lobbyist'
Administrative testimony is defined in 2 C.C.R. Section
18329(d) ( 1) as:
I I ]nfluencing or attempting to influence
administrative action by acting as counsel in,

Margaret W. El1ison, Esq.

appearing as a lvitness in, or providi.ng written
submissions, including ansl{ers to inquiries,
which become part of the record of, any
regulatory or administrative agency proceeding:
(A) Which is conducted as an oPen public hearing
for which public notice is given;
(B) Of which a record is created in a manner
which makes possible the creation of a
transcr ipt; and
(C) With respect to idhich ful1 public access is
provided to such record or transcript and to all
written material which is submitted to become
part of the record,
Thus, the statute recognizes that there is little 
need to
require official disclosure in fully recorded, public
activities. This is parti.cularly so where the Rules of
Professional Conduct applicable to attorneys representing
clients i.n administrative, adjudicatory proceedings, as here,
forbid ex parte contact with the decisionmaker.
The definition of a "lobbying firm" does not
explicitly include the "administrative test j.mony" exception.
Thus, 2 C.C.R. Section 18238.5 might be interpreted to impose a
registration requirement for 1aw firms and their "lobbyist
employers" even though it would appear that the individual
attorneys may avail themselves of the exclusion for
administrative testimony activities in the public hearing
context. It seems unlikely that the drafters of the regulations
(or the legislature. in amending the definition of "lobbying
firm" in covernment code section 82038.5(a) to enact subdivision
(a)(2), a technical clean-up bill), 
intended such an incongruous
result. Indeed. i.f an j.ndividual attorney may engage in
administrative testimony on an unlimited basis without being
required to report, a f i.rm whose individual members engage in
th6 same activities should be treated the same. The paralIeI
wording of Government Code Sectlon S82038.5(a)(2) and Government
Code Section 82039 supports that interpretation' 9/ And, as a
matter of statutory interpretation, "9'lhere the reason is the
same, the rule should be the same. " Ca]. Civ. Code S 3511
(1990). Thus, a firm t^,hose members engage solely in
administrative testimony, 1i,ke that elici'ted in the San Bruno
hearings, should not be required to report as a lobbying f irm.

Second, the intent of the voters in adopting the Act
